titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Otto F. Ege Collection
titlestmt.author: Mary A. Caldera

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday January 18, 2024 at 04:04

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection is comprised of manuscript volumes, manuscript and incunabula leaves and fragments, and bound and unbound partial volumes of manuscripts and print volumes, dating from approximately 1100-1900, collected by educator, collector, and dealer, Otto F. Ege. The collection consists of religious texts, such as bibles, books of hours, prayer books, hymnals, missals, psalters, choir books, and well as histories and literature from Europe, Persia, and Asia. The collection also contains a small amount of material documenting the life and career of Otto F. Ege, including writings, correspondence, photographs, newspaper clippings, and catalogs or advertisements.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Series I is open for research. Series II - Series V are closed until processed.
Some items in Series I are temporarily inaccessible pending preservation actions or further description.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four series: Series I. Manuscript Volumes, circa 1150-circa 1826. Series II. Portfolios, 1100-1935. Series. III. Manuscript and Manuscript Volume Fragments, circa 850-circa 1900. Series IV. Printed Material, circa 1470-1955. Series V. Personal Papers, circa 1900-circa 1950.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Otto F. Ege (1888-1951) was an educator, collector of rare books and manuscripts and, most notably, a dealer, known for removing leaves from manuscript and print volumes for exhibition and sale. In the early 1920s he began to disassemble incomplete medieval manuscripts and incunabula and to sell the leaves, at first as single items but soon in a series of boxed "portfolios" intended to illustrate cultural subjects, including the history of the Bible and "Fifty Original Leaves from Medieval Manuscripts." Ege defended his book breaking on educational grounds, even authoring an article in 1938 titled "I am a Biblioclast."

Otto F. Ege was born in Reading, Pennsylvania, on April 14, 1888, to John Ege and Pauline Ege (nee Wonnberger). He graduated from Reading High School in 1907 and studied design and art education at the Philadelphia Museum School of Art and New York University. In 1920 he moved to Cleveland, Ohio, and began a long career at the Cleveland Institute of Art, holding several positions including instructor, department chair, and dean (1946-1951). He also taught at Western Reserve University (Case Western) and lectured across Ohio, New York, and Pennsylvania. His teaching included the use of book art specimens (or fragments). Ege was an advocate for the teaching and appreciation of Book Arts. He became a noted expert on medieval manuscripts and early modern printed books. He served as tour guide and buyer for several libraries and museum in Ohio. Ege became a renowned collector and dealer.

Scott Gwara, in his extensive study of Ege, characterizes Ege's collecting in three phases. Between 1911 and 1934, Ege collected broadly, building his personal and teaching collection. During this time, Ege sold manuscript fragments taken from damaged or incomplete volumes. Between circa 1935 and circa 1946, Ege established a rare manuscripts and books trade business and sold fragments (from incomplete and intact volumes) in presentation mats and portfolios to museum, libraries, and educational organizations as well as middle-income collectors. The portfolios were compiled to illustrate topics in book history, such as famous bibles, medieval manuscripts and history of type. From about 1947 until his death on June 17, 1951, Ege focused his collecting and business on compiling and selling Original Leaves from Famous Books, exceptional leaves matted and encased as a portfolio set. Ege died before the project was complete, and the compilation and sell of the portfolio was completed by Louise H. Ege (nee Lange), his widow.

Ege's legacy as educator, collector, and dealer is complex. He is recognized as a strong advocate for the education and appreciation of book arts and for creating the market for affordable manuscript and incunabula fragments. He was also criticized for his practice of breaking or disassembling and dispersing (by sell) unique and rare volumes. Proclaiming himself a biblioclast in a 1938 article in Avocations he defended his book-breaking practices for allowing more people the opportunity to see, own, learn about, and appreciate the art in manuscripts, incunabula, and fine books. Many single leaves and fragments collected and sold by Ege are held in North American libraries, their study in digital form has been foundational to the development of digital fragmentology.

Otto F. Ege died on June 17, 1951. He was survived by his wife Lousie H. Ege (nee Lange) and their daughter Elizabeth (Betty). Louise and Betty stewarded Ege's collection, sold or donated parts to various institutions. Ege's grandchildren sold the remainder of the collection to Yale University in 2015.
Porcheddu, Fred. 2007. "Otto F. Ege: Teacher, Collector, and Biblioclast." Art Documentation 26 (1): 4-14.
Gwara, Scott. Otto Ege's Manuscripts: A Study of Ege's Manuscript Collections, Portfolios, and Retail Trade. Cayce, SC: De Brailes Publishing, 2013.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Otto F. Ege Collection. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection is comprised of manuscript volumes, manuscript and incunabula leaves and fragments, and bound and unbound partial volumes of manuscripts and print volumes, dating from approximately 1100-1900, collected by educator, collector, and dealer, Otto F. Ege. The collection consists of religious texts, such as bibles, books of hours, prayer books, hymnals, missals, psalters, choir books, and well as histories and literature from Europe, Persia, and Asia. The collection also contains a small amount of material documenting the life and career of Otto F. Ege, including writings, correspondence, photographs, newspaper clippings, and catalogs or advertisements.

The Ege collection serves to illustrate a thousand years of book history, especially script and illumination, as well as components of the production (such as bindings) and trade of manuscripts and early books.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Description of materials in the Otto F. Ege collection was repurposed from description provided by vendors and notes shared by Scott Gwara. This finding aid will be revised as additional series in the collection are processed and as revisions to description are made by staff.

Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Archival Description Processing Manual.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from John M. Freudenheim, Tom Freudenheim, Jo Freudenheim, Susan Core, Richard Core, and Michael Frisch, via JSJT Holdings, LLC, 2015-2018, with Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, Herman W. Liebert Book Fund, and the John W. Corwith Memorial Fund.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Except as noted below the material was maintained by Ege's heirs. Most of the manuscript volumes in Series I and several single manuscript leaves in Series III were previously on deposit with the Cleveland Museum of Art (CMA). These items are noted in the inventory with CMA numbers, for example "TR12333/2". A few items were previously on deposit with the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, before they were gifted to or purchased by the Beinecke in 2016.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series consists of complete (or almost complete) manuscript volumes (circa 1150-circa 1826), primarily from Europe, that comprised Otto Ege's personal collection.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The volumes in the series are arranged chronologically.

did.unittitle: Defensor, Liber Scintillarum, including Caesarius of Arles, Epistola "Vereor"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Netherlandish; 118 folios. (Ege 44.2000; TR 12828/55)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Greece, probably Constantinople; 158 folios; Rubricated. (Ege 37.2000; TR 12828/38; Not in Census)

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Item is inaccessible as of June 2023 pending preservation housing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northern France, almost certainly diocese of Sens; 95 folios; Perhaps missing all the Easter lessons, possibly a whole quire, given the very short reading assigned for Easter Day, but this may have been in a separate volume. (Ege 33.2000; TR 12828/33; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lower Rhineland; 78 folios; Incomplete. (Ege 45.2000; TR 12828/58; Census II.1938, no. 11)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England, probably Oxford; Unfoliated. (Ege 343.1991; TR 12828/45; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England or France; 28 folios; The pages with the first initial illuminated. (Ege 41.2000; TR 12828/49)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 619 folios on vellum; Incomplete. (Ege 22.1999; TR 12333/4; not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: France, doubtless Paris; 616 folios. (Ege 482.1984; TR12828/42; Not in Census)

did.unittitle: Psalter and Book of Hours (of Unidentified Use)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northeastern France or Flanders (diocese of Cambrai?); 324 folios. (Ege 345.1991; TR 12828/27; Census II.1939, no. 17)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England; 214 folios. (Ege 8.1999; TR 12333/8; De Ricci, Census II.1939, no. 15)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Italy, probably Rome; 176 folios; [Collation: I8 – XXII8.] (Ege 349.1991; TR 12333/18; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 100 folios; [Collation: iii (paper) + I10-VI10 + VII8 + VIII12 + IX10-X10 + ii (paper)]. (Ege 32.2000; TR 12828/29; Not in Census)

did.unittitle: Book of Hours, Use of Rouen

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Normandy, diocese of Rouen, but from a minor center of production; 127 folios; Incomplete; Illuminated. (Ege 9.1999; TR12333/9; Census II.1943, no. 41)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England; 102 folios; (Ege 1026.1988; TR 12828/34; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Netherlands; 121 leaves. (Ege 6.1999; TR 12333/6)

did.unittitle: Book of Hours, Use of Reims

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northern France, doubtless Reims; 123 folios; Illuminated; Rubricated. (Ege 26.1999; TR 12828/59; Not in Census)

did.unittitle: Book of Hours, Use of Paris

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northern France; 130 folios. (Ege 550.1983; TR12828/47; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Probably Austria (likely Vienna), or perhaps southern Germany; 248 folios; Incomplete; Illuminated; Rubricated; Initial A on fol. 217r bears the name of the illuminator, Chunradus Hueber. [152 mm x 105 mm (justification, 94 mm x 62 mm). Single column, 17 lines. Collation: i (vellum flyleaf) + I10 - VII10 + VII9 (stub between 2-3, 7 a singleton) + VIII10 - X10 + XI10 (wants 7; 6 blank) + XII10 (wants 4) + XIII10 - XXV10 + folios missing at end, probably one quire at least + i (vellum flyleaf). Missing 3 leaves at least.] (Ege 10.1999; TR 12333/10; Census II.1945, no. 57)

did.unittitle: Book of Hours, Use of Paris

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northern France, doubtless Le Mans; 135 folios. [Collation: i (paper) + i (medieval vellum) + I12 (kalendar), etc.] (Ege 274.1983; TR 12828/26; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Central France, doubtless Autun; 162 folios; Contemporary binding. (Ege 276.1983; TR 12828/51; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brittany, doubtless Nantes; 148 folios; Incomplete. (Ege 27.1999; TR 12828/60; Not in Census)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northern France; 84 folios; Incomplete; Modern binding. (Ege 5.1999; TR 12333/2; Not in Census)

did.unittitle: Scholastic Encyclopedia on Theological and Related Subjects Arranged Alphabetically

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England; 173 folios; Incomplete; Rubricated. [Collation: I12 + II12 (wants 3 leaves, including 12) + III12 (wants 6-7, apparently) + IV12 + V12 (wants 9) + VI12 – XV12] (Ege 14.1999; TR 12333/14; Not in Census)
